message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://vuink.com/post/fghss-d-dvagreserr-d-dpn/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders-d-dhtml
**Title:** The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (2026 practitioner write-up)
**Found via:** engines-config + measurement search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content to analyze this source.

input.prompt: Extract the full content of this article about AI text-to-speech systems for screen reader users. I need: (1) the publish date, (2) the author and whether this is a personal blog, practitioner write-up, or institutional source, (3) any claims about which TTS engines/voices screen reader power users prefer at high speeds, (4) claims comparing formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) vs neural TTS at high speaking rates, (5) claims about latency, intelligibility, consistency, or naturalness trade-offs, (6) any specific speaking rate figures (words per minute, syllables per second), (7) any concrete recommendations or configuration advice. Quote the relevant passages exactly.

content.content:
# Analysis of AI Text-to-Speech Article for Screen Reader Users

## (1) Publish Date
**2026-01-23** (as shown in the article metadata)

## (2) Author & Source Type
**Author:** Style Pass  
**Source Type:** This appears to be a **personal blog/practitioner write-up** posted on Vuink.com (a content aggregation site). The article is submitted by an individual user rather than an institutional source.

## (3) TTS Engines/Voices Preferred at High Speeds
The article identifies **Eloquence** as the dominant preference among Western English screen reader users:

> "the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003"

The piece emphasizes that blind users favor voices that are "fast, clear, predictable, and efficient" and "sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed."

## (4) Formant vs. Neural TTS Comparison
**Not addressed.** The article does not compare formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) with neural TTS systems.

## (5) Latency, Intelligibility, Consistency, or Naturalness Trade-offs
**Not discussed.** The article mentions that sighted users prefer "natural, conversational" voices while blind users prefer efficiency, but doesn't analyze specific performance trade-offs.

## (6) Specific Speaking Rate Figures
Yes. The article provides concrete rates:

> "can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute"

## (7) Concrete Recommendations or Configuration Advice
**None provided.** The article identifies the problem (Eloquence is outdated and technically unsustainable) but states only that "a better solution is urgently needed" without offering specific recommendations.

content.text: The fetch succeeded but returned somewhat thin content. Let me get more detail to extract additional falsifiable claims.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim every passage that discusses: why Eloquence is hard to replace, the technical/legal/licensing status of Eloquence, why neural/AI TTS voices struggle for screen reader use, anything about responsiveness/latency/instant feedback when navigating, anything about character-by-character or word-by-word reading, anything about the gap between modern AI TTS and what blind users need, and any mention of specific products (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceVox, Vocalizer, etc.). Provide exact quotes with as much surrounding context as possible.

content.content:
# Key Passages on Screen Reader TTS Challenges

## Why Eloquence is Hard to Replace

"Eloquence is a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, it cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge."

"If the sourcecode to Eloquence still exists and can be compiled, even large companies like Apple haven't managed to find or compile it."

"The eloquence libraries also have many known security issues, and anyone using the libraries today is forced to understand and program around them, as Eloquence itself can never be updated or fixed."

## Technical/Legal/Licensing Status

"These stopgap solutions are entirely untenable, and are likely to take us only so far. A better solution is urgently needed."

## User Preferences vs. Modern AI TTS

"Sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible, blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient."

"Blind people getting left out of the explosion of text to speech advancement, and has caused many problems."

## Speed Requirements

"Can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute."

The article mentions NVDA transitioning from 32-bit to 64-bit applications creates compatibility challenges with legacy Eloquence support.

content.text: I now have enough detail. Let me also confirm the responsiveness/latency angle which is relevant to the research question.

input.prompt: Does the article discuss response time, latency, immediacy, or instant audio feedback as a requirement for screen reader voices? Does it explain why modern neural/cloud AI voices are unsuitable due to latency or compute cost or running locally on device? Quote any passage about responsiveness, real-time, local/on-device requirements, or compute. If none exist, say so explicitly.

content.content:
# Response Time and Latency in Screen Reader Voice Requirements

The article **does not discuss** response time, latency, immediacy, or instant audio feedback as specific requirements for screen reader voices.

Similarly, the article **does not explain** why modern neural or cloud-based AI voices might be unsuitable due to latency, compute costs, or local device processing needs.

The article focuses instead on other distinctions: that blind users prefer voices optimized for speed of comprehension (800-900 words per minute) and clarity over naturalness, and that the aging Eloquence voice (last updated 2003) has compatibility and security challenges with modern 64-bit systems. However, these performance concerns relate to software architecture and voice naturalness rather than real-time responsiveness or computational overhead.

content.text: I have sufficient information to extract the claims. This is a practitioner/personal blog write-up that bears directly on angle (2) of the research question regarding screen-reader power users' preferences for older synthesizers at high speed.

claims.claim: Blind/screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer Eloquence (a 2003-era formant-style synthesizer) over modern natural voices because their priorities are speed, clarity, predictability, and efficiency rather than naturalness.
claims.quote: the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003 ... Sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible, blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient.

claims.claim: Screen-reader power users routinely listen at 800-900 words per minute, roughly 3-4x the average speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, which is the speed regime in which the preferred robotic voices remain intelligible.
claims.quote: can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute

claims.claim: The community-preferred high-speed voice is technically obsolete and unmaintainable: Eloquence is a 32-bit binary last compiled in 2003 that requires emulation/bridging on modern systems, has unfixable known security issues, and no one (including Apple) can locate or recompile its source.
claims.quote: Eloquence is a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, it cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge. ... even large companies like Apple haven't managed to find or compile it. The eloquence libraries also have many known security issues ... Eloquence itself can never be updated or fixed.

claims.claim: The author characterizes preferred high-speed screen-reader voices as sounding robotic yet remaining comprehensible at high speed, framing a naturalness-versus-high-speed-intelligibility trade-off where the robotic option wins for power listeners.
claims.quote: sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed

claims.claim: Despite rapid advances in AI/neural TTS, blind users have been excluded from these gains and current stopgaps are unsustainable, implying modern neural voices have not yet matched the high-speed intelligibility of legacy synthesizers.
claims.quote: Blind people getting left out of the explosion of text to speech advancement ... These stopgap solutions are entirely untenable, and are likely to take us only so far. A better solution is urgently needed.

content.text:
Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this practitioner blog post (vuink.com aggregation of a "Style Pass" personal write-up, published 2026-01-23).

Source quality: **blog** — a personal/practitioner write-up republished on a content-aggregation site, not peer-reviewed or institutional, but it provides direct practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening domain, which the research question explicitly requests.

Key findings relevant to the research question (especially angle 2 — formant vs neural at extreme speed):
- Strong corroboration that blind power users prefer the old Eloquence synthesizer for speed/clarity/predictability over natural neural voices.
- A concrete speed figure: 800-900 wpm listening vs 200-250 wpm normal speech (~3-4x).
- Explicit framing of the naturalness-vs-high-speed-intelligibility trade-off ("robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed").

Caveats / limits of this source:
- It does NOT discuss formant-vs-neural mechanisms directly, latency/on-device compute, STOI/STMI metrics, fine-tuning gains, or specific config knobs — so it's useful as community/preference evidence but not for the technical-science or measurement angles (1, 3, 4, 6).
- The 800-900 wpm and "majority of western English blind users" claims are practitioner assertions without cited data, so they should be treated as community knowledge rather than measured fact.
